title: Bee space and hive maintenance
subtitle: Why the 6–9 mm gap controls everything in a managed hive, and how to keep your equipment working with it.
excerpt: The discovery that bees leave a 6 to 9 mm gap clear inside a managed hive (enough to pass through but not enough to fill with comb or propolis) changed beekeeping. Lorenzo Langstroth published the principle in 1851. Every movable-frame hive design since has been built around it.
sourceNotes: L.L. Langstroth, Langstroth on the Hive and Honey-Bee (1853), full text available via Internet Archive. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive. BBKA Technical Bulletin on hive assembly. British Standard BS 1300 (National hive dimensions).

glossaryTerms.definition: The 6 to 9 mm gap that bees leave clear inside a hive. Spaces smaller than 6 mm are filled with propolis; spaces larger than 9 mm are built out with comb. Movable-frame hive design depends on maintaining bee space precisely so that frames can be lifted without damage.

A resinous compound collected from tree buds and sap by forager bees, used inside the hive as a sealant and antimicrobial coating. Bees use it to seal gaps smaller than bee space and to stick frames to the box walls. Sticky, dark-coloured, impossible to remove without a hive tool.

Comb built by bees to fill gaps larger than bee space, typically between the top bars of frames and the crown board, between adjacent frames, or between the floor and the bottom box. Scrape it off during inspections to keep the hive manageable.

content.text: Every time you lift a frame cleanly out of a brood box without tearing comb or crushing bees, you are relying on
. Langstroth observed in the 1850s that bees maintain consistent gaps inside a hive: smaller spaces get filled with
, larger spaces get filled with comb, and the 6 to 9 mm band is left clear as a passage. Build a hive with this gap maintained throughout and the frames stay movable.
What happens when bee space breaks down

A crown board left sitting slightly off square will create a gap on one side larger than 9 mm. The bees build comb there. On the next inspection, lifting the crown board tears open capped honey and brood, triggers an alarm response, and makes a mess. Similarly, frames that are not parallel and properly spaced will be joined by burr comb, because the bees are filling the gaps you inadvertently created.

off the top bars during every inspection, using the flat end of the hive tool. Run the blade along the frame runner rails to clear propolis before replacing the crown board. Frames with bent or misshapen plastic runners let the frame tip and create an uneven gap; replace them.

At the annual clean-out (typically when doing a Bailey comb change in spring), scrape the inside walls of each box back to bare wood and flame them briefly with a blowlamp. This removes propolis build-up, old wax, and any pathogens lodged in the surface.
Why you feel resistance when opening a hive

A hive that has not been opened for several months will be heavily propolised. The bees have sealed every gap they could find. The resistance you feel when lifting the crown board is propolis glue; work the hive tool around the full perimeter before pulling straight up. Jerking the board off without breaking the seal tears open cells and provokes the colony unnecessarily.

Spare boxes, supers, and frames should be stored clean and dry when not in use. Store empty boxes with the frames removed and stacked loosely to allow air circulation. Wax moth will work through stored comb in warm conditions; keep empty supers in a cool outbuilding or freeze any drawn comb you want to preserve for more than three months.
